title: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala
facts: Swami Kesavananda Bharati challenged the Kerala Land Reforms Act 1963 which sought to acquire his mutt's property. The constitutional question was whether Parliament's amending power under Article 368 was unlimited.
issue: Whether Parliament can amend any provision of the Constitution, including Fundamental Rights, without limitation.
holding: By 7:6 majority, the Supreme Court held that while Parliament can amend the Constitution (overruling Golak Nath), it cannot alter or destroy the 'Basic Structure' of the Constitution.
ratio: The Basic Structure doctrine: certain features of the Constitution are so fundamental that no amendment can abrogate them. These include: supremacy of the Constitution, republican and democratic form of government, secular character, separation of powers, federal character, judicial review, free and fair elections, unity and integrity of India.
significance: Landmark constitutional law decision. Placed limits on Parliament's amending power. Has been applied in all subsequent constitutional amendment cases. Prevents 'constitutional suicide.'
key_quote: The amending power is not an unlimited power. It cannot be used to destroy the very features that distinguish it as a Constitution.
iks_connection: Parallels the Dharmic principle that Rājadharma binds even the king — constitutional morality (dharma) constrains even the sovereign amendment power (rāja). The 'Basic Structure' functions like the inviolable core of Ṛta (cosmic order) that even gods cannot violate.
judges: Chief Justice S.M. Sikri, Justices Hegde, Grover, Mukherjea, Jaganmohan Reddy, Khanna, Shelat (majority)

title: Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India
facts: Maneka Gandhi's passport was impounded under the Passports Act 1967. She challenged this on the ground that it violated her right under Article 21. The government claimed it need not give reasons for impounding.
issue: Whether 'procedure established by law' in Article 21 means any procedure laid down by a validly enacted law, or must the procedure itself be just, fair and reasonable.
holding: The word 'law' in Article 21 does not mean any enacted procedure but must be just, fair and reasonable. Articles 14, 19 and 21 form a 'golden triangle' — any law limiting personal liberty must satisfy all three.
ratio: Substantive due process introduced into Indian constitutional law. A procedure that is arbitrary, unfair or unreasonable cannot pass the test of Article 21. The right to travel abroad is within the ambit of personal liberty under Article 21.
significance: Most transformative Article 21 judgment. Opened the door for exponential expansion of fundamental rights. The 'golden triangle' of Articles 14-19-21 became the standard framework for rights adjudication.
key_quote: The law must now be taken to be well settled that Article 21 does not exclude Article 19 and that even if there is a law prescribing a procedure for depriving a person of personal liberty, such law, to be valid, must satisfy the test of Articles 14 and 19 also.
iks_connection: Directly parallels the Dharmic requirement that Danda (punishment/deprivation) must follow just procedure (anusandhanam). Kautilya's insistence that investigation precede punishment maps onto substantive due process. The 'golden triangle' reflects the IKS principle that Dharma, Artha and Kāma must be in balance.

title: Vishaka v. State of Rajasthan
facts: Bhanwari Devi, a social worker in Rajasthan, was gang-raped for trying to prevent a child marriage. Women's rights groups (Vishaka) filed a PIL seeking guidelines against sexual harassment at the workplace.
issue: Whether sexual harassment at workplace violates Articles 14, 15, 19(1)(g) and 21 of the Constitution. What guidelines should govern sexual harassment at workplace pending legislation.
holding: Sexual harassment at workplace is a violation of fundamental rights. The Supreme Court laid down the 'Vishaka Guidelines' as binding law pending legislation.
ratio: Where there is no legislation on a subject, the Supreme Court can issue guidelines having the force of law. Employers have a constitutional duty to provide a safe workplace. Sexual harassment violates the right to work with dignity (Article 21) and gender equality (Articles 14, 15).
significance: Filled a legislative vacuum for 15 years (1997-2013). Vishaka Guidelines were given statutory form by POSH Act 2013 (Prevention, Protection and Prohibition of Sexual Harassment at Workplace). Established precedent for judicial activism in rights enforcement.
key_quote: Each incident of sexual harassment is a violation of 'Fundamental Rights' under Articles 14, 15 and 21 of the Constitution of India.
iks_connection: Reflects Rājadharma's principle of protection of women (strī-rakṣaṇam) — the state and those wielding authority have enhanced duties (Viśeṣa Dharma) toward the vulnerable. The court's issuance of guidelines pending legislation parallels the ancient practice of dharmastha (judge) filling gaps in procedure.
judges: Chief Justice J.S. Verma, Justices Sujata Manohar, B.N. Kirpal

title: ADM Jabalpur v. Shivkant Shukla (Habeas Corpus Case)
facts: During the Emergency (1975-77), thousands were detained under MISA. The question was whether courts could issue writs of habeas corpus when the right to move courts was suspended.
issue: Whether during the Emergency proclaimed under Article 352, a person detained under MISA can challenge their detention before a High Court under Article 226.
holding: By 4:1 majority, the Supreme Court held that during Emergency, the right to challenge detention is suspended. Justice H.R. Khanna dissented — the most famous dissent in Indian constitutional history.
ratio: (Majority) Presidential order suspending enforcement of Articles 14, 21, 22 during Emergency is valid. Courts cannot enquire into the legality of detention. (Khanna J. dissent) Even without Article 21, the executive cannot deprive a person of life or liberty without authority of law — this is a fundamental common law principle independent of the Constitution.
significance: Most controversial Supreme Court judgment. Widely criticised as judicial capitulation to executive authoritarianism. Effectively overruled by: 44th Constitutional Amendment (1978) — Article 21 cannot be suspended during Emergency; Puttaswamy (2017) — retrospectively criticised ADM Jabalpur.
key_quote: Justice Khanna: 'Detention without trial is an anathema to all those who love liberty... Even in the absence of Article 21, the State has no power to deprive a person of his life and liberty without the authority of law.'
iks_connection: The case illustrates the danger when Rājadharma is abandoned — the king (executive) acts like a tyrant (ārta-rāja) instead of a dharma-rāja. Kautilya warned that a king who ignores dharma and relies only on force (daṇḍa) will fall. The Emergency was the constitutional equivalent of adharma.
overruled_by: Partially overruled by implication in Puttaswamy (2017)

title: Justice K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India (Privacy Judgment)
facts: Justice Puttaswamy (retired) challenged the Aadhaar scheme on grounds of right to privacy. The government argued (based on M.P. Sharma and Kharak Singh) that there is no fundamental right to privacy under the Constitution.
issue: Whether the right to privacy is a fundamental right under the Constitution of India.
holding: Unanimously, the 9-judge bench held that privacy is a fundamental right under Article 21 (right to life). It forms an essential component of human dignity and liberty.
ratio: Privacy is the right to be let alone, to control the narrative of one's own life, and to make autonomous choices. It has three aspects: informational privacy, bodily integrity, and decisional autonomy. State may restrict privacy only by: (a) law; (b) legitimate state aim; (c) proportionate means. ADM Jabalpur is expressly stated to be wrong.
significance: One of the most significant constitutional judgments of the 21st century. Foundation for digital rights, data protection, LGBTQ+ rights (relied on in Navtej Johar), and limitation on surveillance. Led to the Personal Data Protection Bill/Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023.
key_quote: Privacy is not a gift of the Constitution but a natural right that pre-exists the Constitution... The Constitution exists to protect inherent rights, not to create them.
iks_connection: The concept that privacy 'pre-exists the Constitution' resonates deeply with the IKS concept of Ātman-based dignity — individual dignity is rooted in the divine self (Ātman/Brahman), antecedent to the state. The Bhagavad Gita's concept of individual soul's inviolability (nainaṃ chindanti śastrāṇi — weapons cannot cut the soul) is the philosophical ancestor of this non-positivist approach to rights.
judges: Chief Justice J.S. Khehar, Justices J. Chelameswar, S.A. Bobde, R.K. Agrawal, R.F. Nariman, A.M. Sapre, D.Y. Chandrachud (authored detailed opinion), S.K. Kaul, S.A. Nazeer

title: Navtej Singh Johar v. Union of India
facts: Petitions challenged Section 377 IPC which criminalised consensual same-sex intercourse between adults. The Supreme Court had earlier upheld Section 377 in Suresh Kumar Koushal (2013), reversing the Delhi High Court's Naz Foundation decision.
issue: Whether Section 377 IPC, insofar as it applies to consensual same-sex intercourse between adults, violates Articles 14, 15, 19, and 21 of the Constitution.
holding: Section 377 is unconstitutional insofar as it applies to consensual same-sex intercourse between adults. It violates the fundamental rights to equality, dignity, privacy and freedom of expression of LGBTQ+ persons.
ratio: Constitutional morality must override social morality. Sexual orientation is an essential and immutable attribute of identity — protected under Article 21 as part of the right to privacy and dignity. LGBTQ+ persons have equal citizenship. The legislature's failure to act does not legitimise unconstitutionality.
significance: Decriminalised homosexuality in India. Landmark for LGBTQ+ rights. Applied Puttaswamy's privacy framework. Established that 'constitutional morality' (Ṛta/Dharma) is superior to popular/social morality.
key_quote: History owes an apology to the members of this community and their families, for the delay in providing redressal for the ignominy and ostracism that they have suffered through the centuries.
iks_connection: Invokes constitutional morality as higher than majoritarian social morality — parallels Ṛta as cosmic order that transcends conventional norms. The concept that some rights are inherent and not granted by the state reflects the Ātman-based dignity framework of Indian philosophy.
judges: Chief Justice Dipak Misra, Justices A.M. Khanwilkar, D.Y. Chandrachud, R.F. Nariman, Indu Malhotra

title: Indra Sawhney v. Union of India (Mandal Commission Case)
facts: The V.P. Singh government implemented the Mandal Commission Report (1980) providing 27% reservation for Other Backward Classes (OBCs) in central government services. This was challenged.
issue: Whether 27% reservation for OBCs is constitutionally valid. Whether the 'creamy layer' should be excluded. Whether Article 16(4) is exhaustive of reservation power.
holding: OBC reservation is valid. Total reservations cannot exceed 50% (50% ceiling rule). The 'creamy layer' — economically advanced OBC members — must be excluded from reservations. Reservations in promotions are not permissible (later modified by Constitutional amendments).
ratio: Reservations are a means to achieving equality, not an end in themselves. The 50% ceiling prevents reservations from becoming the rule rather than the exception. Backwardness must be determined primarily by social and educational criteria, though economic criteria can supplement. The creamy layer concept ensures reservations reach the truly disadvantaged.
significance: Definitive constitutional framework for reservations. The 50% ceiling and creamy layer doctrine are binding on all subsequent reservation policies. Remains the most litigated constitutional judgment in terms of subsequent cases.
key_quote: The power to make reservation is not unlimited. Reservation is an exception and cannot be made the rule.
iks_connection: The tension between Sāmānya Dharma (equal treatment of all) and Viśeṣa Dharma (special treatment for the disadvantaged) is at the heart of reservation law. IKS explicitly recognised that equality of outcome requires different treatment of those in unequal positions — a principle underlying the Arthashastra's special protections for vulnerable groups.

title: Olga Tellis v. Bombay Municipal Corporation
facts: The Bombay Municipal Corporation sought to evict pavement dwellers and slum inhabitants from Bombay under the Maharashtra Slum Areas Act. The petitioners argued this would deprive them of their livelihood.
issue: Whether the right to livelihood is part of the right to life under Article 21. Whether pavement dwellers can be evicted without following a fair procedure.
holding: The right to livelihood is part of the right to life under Article 21. The procedure for eviction must be just, fair and reasonable — mandatory hearing before eviction.
ratio: The sweep of Article 21 includes the right to livelihood. Persons cannot be evicted from public spaces where they live and earn their livelihood without being heard. The right to life includes the right to live with basic human dignity.
significance: First case recognising socio-economic rights under Article 21. Foundation for right to food, right to housing, right to work jurisprudence. Important for urban poor rights litigation.
key_quote: If the right to livelihood is not treated as a part of the constitutional right to life, the easiest way of depriving a person of his right to life would be to deprive him of his means of livelihood.
iks_connection: Directly embodies Rājadharma's principle of dīna-rakṣaṇa (protection of the vulnerable and poor). Arthashastra's provision for protection of artisans and labourers (Book III.13-20) is the IKS ancestor of the right to livelihood.
judges: Chief Justice Y.V. Chandrachud, Justices V.D. Tulzapurkar, A. Varadarajan

title: Minerva Mills Ltd. v. Union of India
facts: The Minerva Mills company challenged the 42nd Constitutional Amendment which (among other things) gave Parliament unlimited amending power and made DPSPs superior to Fundamental Rights.
issue: Whether Sections 4 and 55 of the 42nd Constitutional Amendment which curtailed judicial review and made DPSPs override Fundamental Rights are valid.
holding: Sections 4 and 55 of the 42nd Amendment are unconstitutional. Parliament cannot grant itself unlimited amending power. The balance between Fundamental Rights and DPSPs is part of Basic Structure.
ratio: The Constitution is founded on the bedrock of balance between Fundamental Rights and Directive Principles. Judicial review is a basic feature of the Constitution. Any amendment abrogating this balance violates the Basic Structure.
significance: Confirmed and strengthened Kesavananda Bharati. Restored the balance between rights and directives. Struck down Emergency-era amendments that concentrated power in Parliament.
key_quote: Our Constitution is founded on a nice balance of power among the three organs of the State — Legislature, Executive and Judiciary. It is the function of the Judges to pronounce upon the validity of laws.
iks_connection: The balance between Fundamental Rights (individual Dharma) and DPSPs (collective Rājadharma) mirrors the IKS tension between individual svadharma and collective sādharaṇa dharma. The court's insistence that neither can swallow the other reflects the Puruṣārtha framework's multi-value approach.
judges: Chief Justice Y.V. Chandrachud, Justices A.C. Gupta, N.L. Untwalia, P.S. Kailasam, P.N. Bhagwati

title: Mohori Bibee v. Dharmodas Ghose
facts: Dharmodas Ghose, a minor, mortgaged his property to Brahmo Dutt to secure a loan. His mother (as next friend) sued for cancellation of the mortgage deed on grounds of minority.
issue: Whether a contract entered into by a minor (person below 18 years) is void or merely voidable.
holding: A contract by a minor is void ab initio (void from the beginning), not merely voidable.
ratio: Section 11 of the Indian Contract Act 1872 provides that every person is competent to contract who is of the age of majority. A minor lacks contractual capacity, so any purported contract is void. The mortgagee cannot recover the money advanced.
significance: Fundamental principle of Indian contract law. Binding authority for over 120 years. Distinguished from English law where minor's contracts are voidable. Applied consistently in all subsequent cases involving minors and contracts.
key_quote: The word 'agreement' is defined in Section 2(e) as every promise and set of promises forming the consideration for each other. A minor is incapable of giving a promise which the law can recognise.
iks_connection: The principle of contractual incapacity of minors parallels the Arthashastra's (Book III) recognition that parties to a contract must have legal standing (vyavahāra-śakti) — which excluded children, those of unsound mind, and those under duress.
judges: Lords Macnaghten, Davey, Robertson, and Atkinson

title: Hussainara Khatoon v. Home Secretary, State of Bihar
facts: A newspaper report revealed that thousands of undertrial prisoners in Bihar had been in jail for periods longer than the maximum sentence for their alleged offences. Justice Bhagwati took suo motu cognizance.
issue: Whether prolonged detention of undertrial prisoners violates their fundamental rights under Articles 21 and 22(2) of the Constitution.
holding: Speedy trial is an integral and essential part of the fundamental right to life and liberty under Article 21. The state is obligated to provide free legal aid to accused persons who cannot afford lawyers.
ratio: A procedure that keeps persons in detention for years without trial is not just, fair or reasonable and violates Article 21. The right to speedy trial applies to undertrial prisoners. Free legal aid is a constitutional right, not a charity.
significance: Pioneering use of PIL for prison reform. Led to release of thousands of undertrial prisoners. Foundation for Legal Services Authority Act 1987. Still cited in bail and undertrial prisoner cases.
key_quote: The right to speedy trial is an integral and essential part of the fundamental right to life and liberty enshrined in Article 21.
iks_connection: The Arthashastra (IV.9) emphasises that prolonged pre-trial detention is itself a form of unjust punishment. Vyavahāra procedural principles require expeditious disposal of cases — delayed justice is denied justice. This parallels the Roman maxim 'justice delayed is justice denied.'

title: M.C. Mehta v. Union of India (Oleum Gas Leak Case)
facts: There was an oleum gas leak from the Shriram Foods and Fertilisers plant in Delhi, injuring several persons. M.C. Mehta, an advocate, filed a PIL seeking directions on liability.
issue: Whether the rule in Rylands v. Fletcher (strict liability) applies in India. Whether a new rule of 'absolute liability' should be created for industries engaged in hazardous activities.
holding: India requires a rule stricter than Rylands v. Fletcher. The 'absolute liability' rule: enterprises engaged in inherently dangerous activities are absolutely and non-derogably liable for any harm.
ratio: Indian conditions require a higher standard than the English rule. The 'absolute liability' rule: no defences available (unlike strict liability). The enterprise is absolutely liable to compensate all those affected. This is superior to the Rylands v. Fletcher rule which allows exceptions for act of God, act of stranger, etc.
significance: Created 'absolute liability' doctrine — India's unique contribution to tort law. Subsequently applied in Bhopal Gas Tragedy cases (Union Carbide). Foundation of environmental liability in India. Inspired Hazardous Waste Management Rules.
key_quote: We are of the view that an enterprise which is engaged in a hazardous or inherently dangerous industry which poses a potential threat to the health and safety of the persons working in the factory and residing in the surrounding areas owes an absolute and non-delegable duty to the community.
iks_connection: The absolute liability doctrine resonates with the Arthashastra's provisions on commercial regulation — those who engage in potentially dangerous activities must bear full responsibility (Arthashastra III.19-20 on liability of those who injure others through trade). The IKS concept of Danda proportionate to harm caused supports this strict liability approach.

title: Shreya Singhal v. Union of India
facts: Section 66A of the IT Act 2000 criminalised sending 'offensive' or 'menacing' messages through communication services. Several persons were arrested under this provision for social media posts.
issue: Whether Section 66A of the IT Act 2000 is unconstitutional as violating freedom of speech and expression under Article 19(1)(a).
holding: Section 66A is unconstitutional as it is vague, overbroad, and creates a chilling effect on free speech, violating Article 19(1)(a) without satisfying the Article 19(2) test.
ratio: A law restricting speech must: (a) fall within one of the grounds in Article 19(2); (b) be reasonable, not arbitrary or vague. Section 66A's terms ('offensive', 'menacing', 'grossly' offensive) are not defined and give unbounded discretion to authorities — leading to chilling effect on legitimate speech.
significance: Struck down a widely misused provision. Established the 'chilling effect' doctrine in Indian free speech law. Distinguished between 'advocacy' (protected) and 'incitement' (not protected).
key_quote: What is offensive or grossly offensive is clearly a very subjective and context-dependent test... These terms are so vague, wide and undefined that they are not ascertainable by ordinary persons.
iks_connection: The protection of freedom of speech resonates with Vāk (speech) as sacred in IKS — the Vedas were oral traditions; speech was considered divine. However, this must be balanced with Dharma's prohibition on harmful speech (Manusmriti IV.138 — 'do not speak what is harsh or untruthful or what is hurtful to others').
judges: Justices J. Chelameswar and R.F. Nariman

title: T.M.A. Pai Foundation v. State of Karnataka
facts: T.M.A. Pai Foundation, running educational institutions, challenged state regulations on admissions and fee structures as violating the right to establish and administer educational institutions.
issue: What is the extent of rights of minority and non-minority institutions under Articles 19(1)(g) and 30 of the Constitution with respect to admissions, fee, and administration.
holding: The right to establish and administer an educational institution is a fundamental right. However, the state can regulate to ensure academic excellence and prevent commercialisation. Different standards apply to minority and non-minority institutions.
ratio: Non-minority institutions: state can regulate admissions and fees but cannot take over administration. Minority institutions: have full autonomy under Article 30 subject to minimum academic standards. Capitation fees and profiteering are prohibited.
significance: Definitive framework for regulation of private educational institutions. Has influenced all subsequent education regulation cases.
key_quote: The right to establish and administer an educational institution is a fundamental right guaranteed under Article 19(1)(g), subject to the regulatory power of the State.
iks_connection: The right to education and the existence of autonomous educational institutions (gurukula system) is deeply rooted in IKS. The Arthashastra (I.5) emphasises that the state must support education but should not monopolise it. The tension between state regulation and institutional autonomy mirrors the ancient debate between rājadharma and institutional dharma.

title: Common Cause (A Registered Society) v. Union of India (Passive Euthanasia)
facts: Common Cause, an NGO, filed a PIL seeking legal recognition of 'living wills' (advance medical directives) to allow terminally ill persons to refuse extraordinary life-sustaining treatment.
issue: Whether the right to die with dignity is part of the right to life under Article 21. Whether passive euthanasia (withdrawal of life support) is legal in India.
holding: The right to die with dignity is an intrinsic facet of the right to live with dignity under Article 21. Passive euthanasia is permitted. A person of sound mind may execute an 'advance directive' to refuse treatment.
ratio: Dignity includes the right to refuse medical treatment that merely prolongs dying. A living will executed in sound mind must be respected. Active euthanasia remains prohibited.
significance: First Indian case recognising advance medical directives. Important for medical law, autonomy, and dignified dying. Builds on Gian Kaur's foundation.
key_quote: Dignity in the process of dying is as much a part of the right to life under Article 21 as dignity in living.
iks_connection: The concept of dignified death (Prāyopaveśana — fasting unto death in a dignified manner) has deep roots in IKS. The Jain tradition especially recognises Sallekhanā (voluntary renunciation of body at life's end) as a sacred right. The court's recognition of the right to die with dignity reflects this ancient respect for individual autonomy at death.
judges: Chief Justice Dipak Misra, Justices A.K. Sikri, A.M. Khanwilkar, D.Y. Chandrachud, Ashok Bhushan

title: Mohd. Ahmed Khan v. Shah Bano Begum
facts: Shah Bano, a 62-year-old divorced Muslim woman, sought maintenance from her former husband under Section 125 CrPC. Her husband argued that Muslim personal law did not obligate him to pay maintenance beyond the iddat period.
issue: Whether Section 125 CrPC applies to Muslim women. Whether the Muslim Personal Law (Shariat) Application Act overrides Section 125 CrPC.
holding: Section 125 CrPC applies to all women regardless of religion. A Muslim woman whose husband has divorced her is entitled to maintenance under Section 125.
ratio: Section 125 is a secular provision of social welfare law, not Hindu personal law. It overrides personal law. The husband's obligation to maintain his destitute divorced wife is consistent with Muslim law's obligation of provision during iddat.
significance: Triggered a major political controversy. Led to the Muslim Women (Protection of Rights on Divorce) Act 1986, initially seen as overruling Shah Bano, but subsequently interpreted to provide broader rights in Danial Latifi v. Union of India (2001).
key_quote: There is no conflict between the provisions of Section 125 and those of the Muslim Personal Law on the question of the Muslim husband's obligation to provide maintenance for a divorced wife who is unable to maintain herself.
iks_connection: The tension between personal law and secular law parallels the ancient debate between dharma-śāstra (community-specific dharma) and rāja-dharma (king's law applicable to all). Kautilya's Arthashastra applied uniform commercial and procedural law across all communities while allowing personal law variations.
judges: Chief Justice Y.V. Chandrachud, Justices D.A. Desai, O. Chinnappa Reddy, E.S. Venkataramiah, Rangnath Misra

title: S.R. Bommai v. Union of India
facts: The S.R. Bommai government in Karnataka was dismissed under Article 356 (President's Rule) in 1989. Similar dismissals occurred in other BJP/non-Congress states. The constitutional validity of these dismissals was challenged.
issue: Whether the power under Article 356 to impose President's Rule is justiciable. What are the limitations on its exercise.
holding: The satisfaction of the President under Article 356 is justiciable. Floor test (vote of confidence) must be conducted before imposing President's Rule. President's Rule must have material basis.
ratio: Article 356 is an emergency provision to be used as a last resort, not a tool for political convenience. Federalism, secularism, and democracy are basic features of the Constitution. Dismissal of a state government for extraneous reasons violates Basic Structure.
significance: Drastically curtailed misuse of Article 356 which had been used 100+ times since 1950. Established federalism as a basic feature. Led to dramatic reduction in President's Rule impositions.
key_quote: Secularism is part of the Basic Structure of the Constitution. A government which does not act in accordance with the secular provisions of the Constitution can be dismissed.
iks_connection: S.R. Bommai's protection of federal structure and state autonomy mirrors the Arthashastra's concept of a federal empire (mahajanapada system) where local governance was respected within the overarching dharmic framework of the sovereign. Kautilya recognised that effective governance requires devolution of authority to local units.

title: Vineet Narain v. Union of India (Hawala Case)
facts: A diary recovered from a hawala dealer (Jain brothers) revealed payments to politicians and bureaucrats. Despite CBI investigation orders, the CBI was not taking effective action against senior politicians. Vineet Narain filed a PIL.
issue: Whether the CBI and enforcement agencies were bound to investigate and prosecute all persons named in the diary, including senior politicians. Whether political interference with CBI investigations is permissible.
holding: The CBI and other investigating agencies must act impartially and investigate all persons without consideration of their political status. 'No man is above the law' is a constitutional principle.
ratio: The rule of law requires equal enforcement against powerful and weak alike. Courts can direct investigation agencies to act impartially. CBI must function independently from the government of the day in ongoing investigations.
significance: Established the doctrine of equal enforcement of law against powerful persons. Led to prosecution of several senior politicians. Influenced subsequent CBI independence debates.
key_quote: The CBI is expected to investigate impartially, thoroughly and without being deflected from its task by any consideration of who is being investigated or the fear of the consequences.
iks_connection: The Arthashastra's insistence that the king himself is subject to Danda (punishment) and that officials (amātya) must be held accountable through an independent oversight mechanism (Book II.9 — duties of superintendents) is the IKS ancestor of the equal enforcement doctrine.
